questions.text: I feel a strong pull to be in charge of important initiatives.
I sense when someone is performing confidence they do not actually feel.
I find that celebrations and happy occasions fill me with a joy so strong it's almost painful.
I find that I do not register the difference between a quick hello and a deliberate greeting.
I feel that I have a natural shock absorber for emotional impact.
I tend to choose conferences, workshops, and events based on how they advance my career specifically.
I find that when I receive an unexpected gift, I immediately think of someone who would appreciate it more.
I find that subtext is something I recognize in books more easily than in real life.
I tend to frame every major decision by asking: does this move me closer to where I want to be?
I enjoy adapting to and reinforcing the culture that already exists on a team.
I believe that the moral urgency of suffering exists independently of whether I happen to feel an emotional reaction to it.
I feel that the wellbeing of those around me is ultimately more important than my own wellbeing.
I find it difficult to dial down my emotional intensity once it has ramped up.
I ramp up effort as a project progresses, letting early stages stay loose and exploratory.
I hold the view that my free time is a resource that should flow toward whoever needs it most.
I believe that too many habits can make life mechanical and rob it of spontaneity.
I find that my emotional life is so vivid it sometimes feels like I'm living more intensely than my circumstances warrant.
I have noticed that I respond to others' emotional pain with analysis rather than with feeling.
I see my presence in a group as primarily about providing direction.
When I am near someone in deep pain, their suffering exists in my awareness as a moral weight that requires a response.
I hold the view that the moderate volume of my emotions is not numbness — it is a different kind of richness.
I review my calendar at the start of each day to set my intentions.
I find myself reading between the lines in most conversations.
I hold the view that composure during distressing situations is the quality that creates space for careful deliberation in circumstances where haste would be harmful.
I find that I navigate social situations best when the rules are clearly defined.
I stay available throughout the day because the best conversations happen unexpectedly.
I believe that the moral significance of suffering creates a responsibility that is both immediate and enduring.
I experience the onset of any emotion as a gradual dawn rather than a sudden blaze.
I tend to choose my battles based on what matters most to my own goals.
I notice the precise moment when a group conversation becomes dominated by one person.
I think that analytical detachment during emotionally charged moments is a kind of service that is undervalued because it does not look like caring.
I feel that buy-in justifies taking extra time for collective decision-making.
I feel that consistency is what transforms average effort into exceptional outcomes.
I consider the downstream effects of my choices on future commitments before deciding.
I feel that my decisiveness prevents wasted effort.
I naturally gravitate toward connecting people and letting them find their own roles.
